summary: Jeff Atwood examines Google's 2007 initiative to warn users about malware-infected websites directly in search results. He analyzes the technical mechanisms behind a specific malicious site and discusses the ethical responsibility that comes with Google's dominant position as the internet's de-facto start page. While applauding the effort, he questions why Google keeps malware sites indexed with warnings rather than delisting them entirely, as competitors do. He concludes that search engines have a unique responsibility to help combat web-based malware, which affects one in ten webpages.
key_points: Google implemented interstitial warnings for malware sites rather than delisting them, unlike competitors who removed such sites entirely
Users determined to see 'dancing bunnies' will bypass any number of warnings - security measures can only do so much against user intent
Legitimate websites can become malware vectors through compromised servers, unsanitized user HTML, or questionable advertising networks
Google's research found that one in ten webpages contains malicious code, making this a massive-scale problem
With great search dominance comes great responsibility - doing nothing would be irresponsible given Google's position as the internet's start page
money_quotes: To do nothing – to let these purely evil sites show up in Google results with no warning whatsoever – would be irresponsible.
Users will find a way to see the dancing bunnies if they really, really want to, no matter how many warnings and barriers you blast in front of them.
If you accept that Google wields the immense power of being the de-facto start page for the internet, then maybe this kind of policing effort comes with the territory.
Despite the individual ethics of the person using that one computer, a compromised computer will be used for attacks and spam against everyone.
I don't think it's fair to call this Google policing the web; it's just good, ethical business to filter out the evil.
It's scary how many ways this can happen.
key_insight: Search engines bear ethical responsibility to protect users from malware, not as censorship but as a natural extension of their role as gatekeepers to the internet.
